tasks.title: Warm-up: Install kube-prometheus-stack with Helm and Verify Pod Status
tasks.goal: Install kube-prometheus-stack into the monitoring namespace using Helm, then confirm that all four core components (Prometheus, Grafana, Node Exporter, kube-state-metrics) are in the Running state.
tasks.prompt:
1. Add the prometheus-community Helm repository and update it to the latest index.
2. Install the kube-prometheus-stack chart as a release named prometheus into the monitoring namespace. Include the option to create the namespace automatically if it does not exist.
3. Run kubectl get pods -n monitoring to list the Pods, and identify which Pod names correspond to each of the four core components (Prometheus, Grafana, Node Exporter, kube-state-metrics). Record the names.
4. Run the command to list installed Helm releases and confirm that the prometheus release shows a DEPLOYED status.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Add the Helm repository (fill in the blanks)
helm repo add prometheus-community ____
helm repo ____

# 2. Install the chart (fill in the blanks)
helm install prometheus prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack \
  --namespace ____ \
  ____

# 3. List Pods (fill in the blank)
kubectl get pods ____ monitoring

# 4. List Helm releases (fill in the blank)
helm ____ -n monitoring
tasks.solution:
# 1. Add the Helm repository
helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update

# 2. Install the chart
helm install prometheus prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack \
  --namespace monitoring \
  --create-namespace

# 3. List Pods
kubectl get pods -n monitoring
# Expected output (names may vary by version):
# prometheus-grafana-...                Running
# prometheus-kube-prometheus-operator-... Running
# prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus-0 Running
# prometheus-kube-state-metrics-...      Running
# prometheus-prometheus-node-exporter-... Running  (one per node)

# 4. List Helm releases
helm list -n monitoring
# Expected output: prometheus release shown with STATUS=deployed
tasks.explanation: helm repo add registers a chart repository URL under a local alias (prometheus-community), and helm repo update downloads the latest chart index from that repository. The first argument to helm install is the release name (prometheus); the second is the chart address (alias/chart-name). Without the --create-namespace flag, the monitoring namespace must already exist, so including it is the safer approach. A single installation of kube-prometheus-stack deploys the Prometheus Operator, Prometheus instance, Alertmanager, Grafana, Node Exporter, and kube-state-metrics all at once — this is the core value of a Helm chart: managing multiple components as a single unit.
hints: The URL for helm repo add is https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts. Omitting https will cause the repository registration to fail.

Immediately after installation, many Pods will show ContainerCreating while images are being pulled. Wait 1-2 minutes and re-run kubectl get pods -n monitoring, or use kubectl get pods -n monitoring -w to watch the status update live.

If helm list returns nothing, you have likely omitted the -n monitoring option. helm list only queries the current namespace (usually default) by default, so you must specify --all-namespaces or -n monitoring explicitly.

tasks.title: Core Exercise 1: Access the Prometheus UI via port-forward and Run PromQL Queries
tasks.goal: Use kubectl port-forward to reach the Prometheus web UI (port 9090), run two PromQL queries directly, and verify that the Node Exporter and kube-state-metrics targets show an UP status.
tasks.prompt:
1. Use kubectl port-forward to expose the Prometheus service in the monitoring namespace on local port 9090.
   - Service name: prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus
   - Service port: 9090
2. Open localhost:9090 in a browser, go to the Graph tab, and run the following two PromQL queries one at a time. Observe the results.
   - Query A: The number of running Pods per namespace across the entire cluster
   - Query B: The number of available CPU cores on the current node
3. Navigate to Status > Targets and confirm that the node-exporter and kube-state-metrics targets show a state of UP.
4. Return to the terminal and stop the port-forward (Ctrl+C).
tasks.starter:
# 1. Prometheus UI port-forward (fill in the blanks)
kubectl port-forward -n ____ svc/____ ____:____

# --- Open localhost:9090 in a browser ---

# 2-A. Running Pod count per namespace (fill in the blank - complete the PromQL)
# Hint: aggregate kube_pod_container_status_running by the namespace label
sum by (____) (____)

# 2-B. CPU core count per node (fill in the blank - complete the PromQL)
# Hint: use machine_cpu_cores or count(node_cpu_seconds_total{mode="idle"}) by (instance)
____
tasks.solution:
# 1. Prometheus UI port-forward
kubectl port-forward -n monitoring svc/prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus 9090:9090
# To run in the background (Linux/macOS), append & at the end:
# kubectl port-forward -n monitoring svc/prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus 9090:9090 &

# 2-A. Running Pod count per namespace
sum by (namespace) (kube_pod_container_status_running)
# Expected result: namespace="monitoring" -> 6-8, namespace="kube-system" -> several, etc.

# 2-B. CPU core count per node
count(node_cpu_seconds_total{mode="idle"}) by (instance)
# Alternative: machine_cpu_cores
# Expected result: {instance="<node-IP>:9100"} -> 2 (for a 2-core node)

# 3. Check Status > Targets
# Navigate to localhost:9090/targets in the browser
# serviceMonitor/monitoring/prometheus-kube-prometheus-node-exporter and
# serviceMonitor/monitoring/prometheus-kube-state-metrics should both show State=UP

# 4. Stop port-forward
# Ctrl+C (or if running in background: kill %1)
tasks.explanation: kubectl port-forward svc/<service-name> <local-port>:<service-port> creates a temporary tunnel to a cluster service. This lets you access internal services from a local browser without needing a LoadBalancer or Ingress — indispensable for debugging and validation. In PromQL, sum by (namespace) is an aggregation function that sums values grouped by the specified label dimension. The kube_pod_container_status_running metric is collected by the kube-state-metrics exporter; a value of 1 means the container is running. The UP/DOWN status on the Targets page indicates whether Prometheus can actually reach and scrape the exporter — if a target is DOWN, the first things to check are the ServiceMonitor configuration and any network policies.
hints: After running the port-forward command you should see 'Forwarding from 127.0.0.1:9090 -> 9090'. Keep that terminal tab open while you use the browser — closing it or pressing Ctrl+C will drop the connection.
After entering a PromQL query, click the 'Execute' button rather than pressing Enter. On the Graph tab, set the time range to '1h' to see a time-series graph.

If some targets show DOWN on the Targets page, that is not necessarily a problem as long as node-exporter and kube-state-metrics are UP — the core infrastructure metrics are being collected correctly. Some targets may show as DOWN briefly right after installation; refresh after 2-3 minutes.

tasks.title: Core Exercise 2: Access Grafana via port-forward, Decode the Password Secret, and Explore Dashboards
tasks.goal: Use kubectl port-forward to reach Grafana (port 3000), decode the admin password directly from a Secret, log in, and locate the PromQL query powering a CPU usage panel in a pre-built dashboard.
tasks.prompt:
1. Use kubectl port-forward to expose the Grafana service in the monitoring namespace on local port 3000.
   - Service name: prometheus-grafana
   - Internal service port: 80 (mapped to local port 3000)
2. Before logging in, decode the Grafana admin password from a Secret.
   - Secret name: prometheus-grafana (monitoring namespace)
   - Extract the admin-password field using jsonpath and base64-decode it.
3. Log in to localhost:3000 with the username admin and the decoded password.
4. Open the 'Kubernetes / Compute Resources / Node (Pods)' dashboard from the left-side Dashboards menu, click Edit on one of the CPU Usage panels, and record the PromQL query shown in the Metrics browser.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Grafana port-forward (fill in the blanks)
kubectl port-forward -n ____ svc/____ ____:____

# 2. Decode the Grafana admin password (fill in the blanks)
kubectl get secret -n monitoring ____ \
  -o jsonpath="{.data.____}" | base64 ____

# --- Open localhost:3000 in a browser ---
# Username: admin
# Password: the decoded value from above
tasks.solution:
# 1. Grafana port-forward
kubectl port-forward -n monitoring svc/prometheus-grafana 3000:80

# 2. Decode the admin password from the Secret
kubectl get secret -n monitoring prometheus-grafana \
  -o jsonpath="{.data.admin-password}" | base64 --decode
# The default value is usually 'prom-operator', but it may have been changed during installation — always check directly
# On Windows PowerShell:
# $encoded = kubectl get secret -n monitoring prometheus-grafana -o jsonpath="{.data.admin-password}"
# [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetString([System.Convert]::FromBase64String($encoded))

# 3. Log in to localhost:3000 with admin / <decoded password>

# 4. Dashboard navigation path
# Dashboards > Browse > kubernetes-mixin folder (or General) >
# 'Kubernetes / Compute Resources / Node (Pods)'
# Click a CPU Usage panel > click the panel title > Edit
# Example PromQL shown in the Metrics browser:
# sum(node_namespace_pod_container:container_cpu_usage_seconds_total:sum_irate{node="$node"})
#   by (pod)
tasks.explanation: Each panel in Grafana is a PromQL query (or multiple queries) sent to Prometheus under the hood. Viewing a query in the Edit view makes it clear that a dashboard is not magic — it is PromQL results rendered visually. The reason this exercise has you decode the Secret directly is to reinforce two things: the practical use of base64 --decode covered in an earlier lecture, and the operational habit of reading credentials from cluster Secrets rather than looking for hardcoded defaults in documentation. Note that when a field name under data contains a hyphen (-) in a jsonpath expression, it must be wrapped in quotes.
hints: The port-forward is written against the service's internal port. The prometheus-grafana service listens on port 80 inside the cluster, so 3000:80 is correct. Verify by running kubectl get svc -n monitoring prometheus-grafana and checking the PORT column.
If base64 --decode does not work, try base64 -d (macOS uses -D instead of --decode by default). On Windows PowerShell, use the PowerShell approach shown in the solution.

If the dashboard list is empty, click the Dashboards icon (four squares) in the left sidebar and type 'Kubernetes' in the search box. The pre-built dashboards are located in the kubernetes-mixin or Kubernetes folder.

tasks.title: Applied: kubectl logs Options Practice + Write a ServiceMonitor YAML
tasks.goal: Use the key options of kubectl logs (-f, --previous, -c, --since) to query logs hands-on, then write a ServiceMonitor resource YAML from scratch to understand how an application's metrics are connected to Prometheus.
tasks.prompt:
Part A — kubectl logs practice
1. Find and record the name of the Prometheus Pod in the monitoring namespace.
   - Pod name pattern: prometheus-prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus-0
2. Print the last 20 lines of that Pod's logs using the --tail option.
3. Stream live logs from the prometheus container in the same Pod for 5 seconds using the -f option, then stop with Ctrl+C.
4. Print only the logs from the last 2 minutes using the --since option.

Part B — Write a ServiceMonitor YAML
Complete the ServiceMonitor manifest (myapp-monitor.yaml) with no blanks remaining, satisfying the following requirements:
- Name: myapp-monitor / Namespace: default
- Label release: prometheus so Prometheus can discover it
- Target Service label: app=myapp
- Scrape port: web (port name), path: /metrics, interval: 30s
tasks.starter:
# Part A
# 1. Find the Prometheus Pod name
kubectl get pods -n monitoring | grep prometheus-prometheus

# 2. Print last 20 lines (fill in the blank)
kubectl logs -n monitoring <pod-name> ____

# 3. Live logs from the prometheus container (fill in the blanks)
kubectl logs -n monitoring <pod-name> ____ prometheus ____

# 4. Logs from the last 2 minutes (fill in the blank)
kubectl logs -n monitoring <pod-name> ____ 2m

# Part B — myapp-monitor.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: ____
metadata:
  name: myapp-monitor
  namespace: default
  labels:
    ____: ____
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      ____: ____
  endpoints:
    - port: ____
      path: ____
      interval: ____
tasks.solution:
# Part A
# 1. Find the Prometheus Pod name
kubectl get pods -n monitoring | grep prometheus-prometheus
# Output example: prometheus-prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus-0   2/2   Running

# 2. Print last 20 lines
kubectl logs -n monitoring prometheus-prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus-0 --tail=20
# (If the Pod has more than one container, add -c prometheus)

# 3. Live logs from the prometheus container
kubectl logs -n monitoring prometheus-prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus-0 -c prometheus -f
# Stop with Ctrl+C after 5 seconds

# 4. Logs from the last 2 minutes
kubectl logs -n monitoring prometheus-prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus-0 --since=2m

# Part B — myapp-monitor.yaml
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: ServiceMonitor
metadata:
  name: myapp-monitor
  namespace: default
  labels:
    release: prometheus
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: myapp
  endpoints:
    - port: web
      path: /metrics
      interval: 30s

# Apply (YAML syntax can be validated even without an actual myapp Service)
# kubectl apply -f myapp-monitor.yaml
tasks.explanation: Quick reference for key kubectl logs options: --tail=N prints only the last N lines; -f streams logs in real time; --previous (-p) shows logs from the container before its last restart; --since=<duration> filters to logs within a recent window (e.g., 1h, 30m, 2m). When a Pod has multiple containers, specify the target with -c <container-name>. The two most important things in a ServiceMonitor are: first, the release: prometheus label in metadata.labels — kube-prometheus-stack is configured to discover only ServiceMonitors carrying this label, so leaving it out means the resource is defined but never actually scrapes anything. Second, spec.selector.matchLabels must match the labels on the actual Service, and spec.endpoints.port must match the port name (not the port number) defined in that Service's ports[].name field.
hints: If kubectl logs returns 'a container name must be specified for pod ... because it has multiple containers', the Pod has more than one container. Run kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n monitoring and look in the Containers section for the names, then add -c <name>. Prometheus Pods typically have two containers: prometheus and config-reloader.

The apiVersion for ServiceMonitor is monitoring.coreos.com/v1. Using apps/v1 or v1 will produce a 'no matches for kind ServiceMonitor' error. The Prometheus Operator must be installed for this CRD to exist.

If the target does not appear in Prometheus Targets after applying myapp-monitor.yaml, check two things: (1) is metadata.labels.release set to prometheus? (2) does spec.selector.matchLabels match the labels on an actual existing Service? If the Targets page shows 'no such host', the Service simply does not exist yet.
